exercises.title: Identify gRPC Spring Boot annotations
exercises.instructions:
Match each annotation/class to its role in Spring Boot gRPC:
A: @GrpcService
B: @GrpcClient("payment-service")
C: responseObserver.onNext(response)
D: responseObserver.onCompleted()
E: StreamObserver<PaymentRequest> requestObserver
F: grpc.server.port=9090 in application.yml
exercises.expected_solution:
A: @GrpcService
   Marks a class as a gRPC service implementation
   Spring Boot registers it automatically with the gRPC server
   Class must extend the generated ServiceImplBase
   Equivalent: @RestController but for gRPC

B: @GrpcClient("payment-service")
   Injects a gRPC client stub into a Spring bean
   The string is the client name configured in application.yml
   Field type: PaymentServiceGrpc.PaymentServiceBlockingStub
   Equivalent: @FeignClient for REST APIs

C: responseObserver.onNext(response)
   Sends one response message to the client
   Unary RPC: call exactly once
   Server streaming: call multiple times
   Must be called before onCompleted()

D: responseObserver.onCompleted()
   Signals: stream is finished, no more responses
   Must be called after all onNext() calls
   Equivalent: HTTP response.close() / end-of-response
   If not called: client hangs waiting forever!

E: StreamObserver<PaymentRequest> requestObserver
   Client-side streaming or bidirectional: receives messages from client
   Server implements this for client-side streaming
   requestObserver.onNext() called by client framework for each message

F: grpc.server.port=9090
   gRPC server port (default 9090, separate from HTTP 8080)
   Both can run in same Spring Boot app simultaneously
   HTTP/2 only: gRPC doesn't work on HTTP/1.1

test_cases.expected:
A: @GrpcService
   Marks a class as a gRPC service implementation
   Spring Boot registers it automatically with the gRPC server
   Class must extend the generated ServiceImplBase
   Equivalent: @RestController but for gRPC

B: @GrpcClient("payment-service")
   Injects a gRPC client stub into a Spring bean
   The string is the client name configured in application.yml
   Field type: PaymentServiceGrpc.PaymentServiceBlockingStub
   Equivalent: @FeignClient for REST APIs

C: responseObserver.onNext(response)
   Sends one response message to the client
   Unary RPC: call exactly once
   Server streaming: call multiple times
   Must be called before onCompleted()

D: responseObserver.onCompleted()
   Signals: stream is finished, no more responses
   Must be called after all onNext() calls
   Equivalent: HTTP response.close() / end-of-response
   If not called: client hangs waiting forever!

E: StreamObserver<PaymentRequest> requestObserver
   Client-side streaming or bidirectional: receives messages from client
   Server implements this for client-side streaming
   requestObserver.onNext() called by client framework for each message

F: grpc.server.port=9090
   gRPC server port (default 9090, separate from HTTP 8080)
   Both can run in same Spring Boot app simultaneously
   HTTP/2 only: gRPC doesn't work on HTTP/1.1

hints: @GrpcService = @RestController for gRPC
onCompleted() is mandatory — never forget it
Port 9090: gRPC; 8080: HTTP — both can coexist

exercises.title: Complete the Spring Boot gRPC service
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blanks in a Spring Boot gRPC service implementation.

blanks.hint: signal end of stream — ALWAYS required

hints: @GrpcService: net.devh.boot.grpc.server.service.GrpcService
ImplBase: generated by protoc from the .proto service definition
onCompleted(): ALWAYS call — client hangs if missing

exercises.title: application.yml gRPC configuration
exercises.instructions:
Write the Spring Boot gRPC configuration:
1. Server: port 9090, max message size 4MB
2. Client 'razorpay-payment': address static://localhost:9090, plaintext (no TLS)
3. Client 'zerodha-trade': address static://trade-service:9091
4. Explain: how @GrpcClient resolves the address
exercises.expected_solution:
# application.yml — Spring Boot gRPC configuration
# pom.xml deps:
# net.devh:grpc-server-spring-boot-starter:2.15.0.RELEASE
# net.devh:grpc-client-spring-boot-starter:2.15.0.RELEASE

grpc:
  server:
    port: 9090                    # gRPC server port (separate from HTTP 8080)
    max-inbound-message-size: 4MB # max request message size
    max-inbound-metadata-size: 8KB

  client:
    razorpay-payment:             # name used in @GrpcClient("razorpay-payment")
      address: static://localhost:9090
      negotiation-type: plaintext  # TLS disabled (dev only!)
      deadline-duration: 5s        # request timeout

    zerodha-trade:                # name used in @GrpcClient("zerodha-trade")
      address: static://trade-service:9091
      negotiation-type: plaintext
      deadline-duration: 10s       # longer for trading operations

# HOW @GrpcClient RESOLVES:
# @GrpcClient("razorpay-payment")
# private PaymentServiceGrpc.PaymentServiceBlockingStub stub;
# → Looks up grpc.client.razorpay-payment.address
# → Creates managed channel to localhost:9090
# → Creates blocking stub from that channel
# → Injects stub into the field
# Note: "static://" means fixed address (no service discovery)
# For Kubernetes: use "discovery:///service-name" with Spring Cloud LoadBalancer

public class GrpcConfigDoc {
    public static void main(String[] a) {
        System.out.println("gRPC server: port 9090 | client: @GrpcClient resolves via yml");
    }
}
hints: grpc.client.NAME: name matches @GrpcClient("NAME") exactly
plaintext: dev only — production always uses TLS
static://: fixed address; discovery:///: service discovery

exercises.title: gRPC service with Spring dependency injection
exercises.instructions:
Write a @GrpcService that uses Spring beans:
1. RazorpayPaymentService @GrpcService: inject PaymentRepository + AuditLogger
2. processPayment(): use repository to save, logger to audit
3. @SpringBootTest: service beans are wired correctly
4. Show: @GrpcService works with @Autowired / constructor injection
hints: @GrpcService: Spring-managed bean — full DI support
Constructor injection: preferred over @Autowired fields
@GrpcService and @Service coexist in same Spring context

exercises.title: gRPC client stub usage
exercises.instructions:
Write a gRPC client service:
1. TradeClientService: @GrpcClient injects BlockingStub for zerodha-trade
2. placeOrder(symbol, qty, type): call stub.placeOrder(request)
3. Handle StatusRuntimeException (gRPC equivalent of HTTP errors)
4. @SpringBootTest: client setup documented, error handling verified
hints: StatusRuntimeException: gRPC equivalent of HTTP status codes
Status.Code: UNAVAILABLE, DEADLINE_EXCEEDED, NOT_FOUND, PERMISSION_DENIED
@GrpcClient: real usage — replaced by constructor injection for testability here

exercises.title: gRPC error handling with Status codes
exercises.instructions:
Write proper gRPC error handling:
1. PaymentGrpcService: validates request, throws INVALID_ARGUMENT if bad
2. onError: use Status.INVALID_ARGUMENT.withDescription().asRuntimeException()
3. NOT_FOUND: when payment doesn't exist
4. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): valid request succeeds, invalid throws correct status
hints: Status.INVALID_ARGUMENT.asRuntimeException(): correct way to error a gRPC call
obs.onError() + return: stop processing (don't call onNext after onError)
TestObserver: captures onNext/onError/onCompleted for assertions

exercises.title: gRPC interceptor for logging
exercises.instructions:
Write a gRPC server interceptor for request logging:
1. LoggingInterceptor implements ServerInterceptor
2. Log: method name, start time, end time, status code
3. Register via @GrpcGlobalServerInterceptor
4. @SpringBootTest: interceptor fires on every gRPC call
hints: @GrpcGlobalServerInterceptor: applied to ALL gRPC services automatically
ForwardingServerCall: intercept at close() to get final status code and latency
Metadata headers: contains authentication tokens, tracing headers

exercises.title: gRPC metadata (headers) propagation
exercises.instructions:
Use gRPC Metadata to pass request headers:
1. Extract X-Merchant-Id from gRPC Metadata (like HTTP headers)
2. Pass merchant context to service logic
3. Client interceptor: add auth token to outgoing Metadata
4. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): metadata extraction, client adds token
hints: Metadata.Key.of: defines a header key (like HTTP header name)
Context.key(): gRPC thread-local for passing values through interceptor chains
ForwardingClientCall: intercept client calls to add auth headers

exercises.title: gRPC health check with Spring Actuator
exercises.instructions:
Implement gRPC health checking:
1. HealthCheckService: SERVING/NOT_SERVING/UNKNOWN
2. Integrate with Spring Actuator health
3. Respond to gRPC health protocol (grpc.health.v1.Health)
4. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): health check returns SERVING, actuator reports UP
hints: HealthCheckResponse.ServingStatus: SERVING, NOT_SERVING, UNKNOWN
GrpcHealthIndicator: standard Spring Actuator pattern for custom health checks
gRPC health protocol: clients can probe server health before sending requests

exercises.title: gRPC vs REST decision analysis
exercises.instructions:
For each Zerodha/Razorpay scenario, choose gRPC or REST and explain:
A: Zerodha Kite web app (browser) fetching portfolio data
B: Internal microservice: OrderService → TradeExecutionService (10K req/s)
C: Razorpay public payment API (merchants integrate from any language)
D: Real-time stock price updates pushed to mobile app
E: Admin dashboard API (used by 10 internal employees)
exercises.expected_solution:
A: Browser fetching portfolio → REST
   Browsers: native gRPC not supported (requires gRPC-Web proxy)
   REST: direct browser fetch() API works perfectly
   GraphQL subscription: better than REST for real-time updates
   Rule: browser-facing → REST or GraphQL

B: OrderService → TradeExecutionService (10K req/s) → gRPC
   Same org, both Java services: gRPC is ideal
   10,000 req/s: gRPC's HTTP/2 multiplexing + binary Protobuf handles this easily
   REST at 10K/s: JSON parsing overhead becomes significant
   Strong contract: .proto file shared between teams
   Rule: high-throughput internal microservice → gRPC

C: Razorpay public API (any language) → REST
   Public API: merchants use Python, PHP, Ruby, Node
   gRPC: requires protobuf client generation per language
   REST: universal — curl works, every language has HTTP client
   Razorpay actually uses REST: https://razorpay.com/docs/api
   Rule: public API for external developers → REST

D: Real-time stock prices to mobile → gRPC server streaming OR WebSocket
   Server streaming gRPC: server pushes price updates to client continuously
   Works on mobile (iOS/Android gRPC clients available)
   vs WebSocket: gRPC is more structured (typed messages)
   Zerodha Kite: uses WebSocket for mobile (legacy), gRPC for new clients
   Rule: real-time server push + mobile → gRPC streaming or WebSocket

E: Admin dashboard (10 users) → REST (or GraphQL)
   Low traffic: performance irrelevance (10 users, not 10K)
   Flexibility: admin needs ad-hoc queries (GraphQL wins)
   Developer experience: REST + Swagger UI is simplest to build
   Rule: internal low-traffic dashboard → REST or GraphQL (simplicity wins)

test_cases.expected:
A: Browser fetching portfolio → REST
   Browsers: native gRPC not supported (requires gRPC-Web proxy)
   REST: direct browser fetch() API works perfectly
   GraphQL subscription: better than REST for real-time updates
   Rule: browser-facing → REST or GraphQL

B: OrderService → TradeExecutionService (10K req/s) → gRPC
   Same org, both Java services: gRPC is ideal
   10,000 req/s: gRPC's HTTP/2 multiplexing + binary Protobuf handles this easily
   REST at 10K/s: JSON parsing overhead becomes significant
   Strong contract: .proto file shared between teams
   Rule: high-throughput internal microservice → gRPC

C: Razorpay public API (any language) → REST
   Public API: merchants use Python, PHP, Ruby, Node
   gRPC: requires protobuf client generation per language
   REST: universal — curl works, every language has HTTP client
   Razorpay actually uses REST: https://razorpay.com/docs/api
   Rule: public API for external developers → REST

D: Real-time stock prices to mobile → gRPC server streaming OR WebSocket
   Server streaming gRPC: server pushes price updates to client continuously
   Works on mobile (iOS/Android gRPC clients available)
   vs WebSocket: gRPC is more structured (typed messages)
   Zerodha Kite: uses WebSocket for mobile (legacy), gRPC for new clients
   Rule: real-time server push + mobile → gRPC streaming or WebSocket

E: Admin dashboard (10 users) → REST (or GraphQL)
   Low traffic: performance irrelevance (10 users, not 10K)
   Flexibility: admin needs ad-hoc queries (GraphQL wins)
   Developer experience: REST + Swagger UI is simplest to build
   Rule: internal low-traffic dashboard → REST or GraphQL (simplicity wins)

hints: Browser → REST (gRPC-Web is complex)
High-throughput internal → gRPC
Public API → REST (universal)

exercises.title: Complete Zerodha trade gRPC service
exercises.instructions:
Build complete Zerodha trade gRPC service:
1. @GrpcService with Spring DI (OrderRepo + TradeAuditLogger)
2. placeOrder: validate, save, audit, respond
3. getOrder: lookup, NOT_FOUND if missing
4. Interceptor: log method + status + latency
5. @SpringBootTest (4 tests): place order, not found, invalid arg, interceptor fires
hints: ifPresentOrElse: clean Optional handling for gRPC NOT_FOUND
audit.count.get() after placeOrder: proves audit was called
TestObserver: captures all three gRPC callbacks (onNext, onError, onCompleted)

exercises.title: gRPC client with deadline and retry
exercises.instructions:
Write a resilient gRPC client:
1. DeadlineGrpcClient: calls with 5-second deadline (timeout)
2. On DEADLINE_EXCEEDED: retry once with fresh deadline
3. On UNAVAILABLE: throw immediately (don't retry — server is down)
4. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): success, deadline exceeded+retry, unavailable throws
hints: DEADLINE_EXCEEDED: retry once (transient timeout)
UNAVAILABLE: don't retry (server is down — retry will also fail)
AtomicInteger: count calls to verify retry happened exactly once

exercises.title: Complete Razorpay payment gRPC service
exercises.instructions:
Build complete Razorpay payment gRPC service with full error handling:
1. processPayment: validate, save, emit event, return response
2. getPaymentStatus: lookup, NOT_FOUND if missing
3. cancelPayment: FAILED_PRECONDITION if already settled
4. Logging interceptor counts calls per method
5. @SpringBootTest (4 tests): process, status, cancel-settled, interceptor
hints: FAILED_PRECONDITION: business rule violated (settled payment can't be cancelled)
ifPresentOrElse: clean Optional → gRPC NOT_FOUND pattern
store.updateStatus: simulate external settlement for cancel test

exercises.title: gRPC client load balancing
exercises.instructions:
Design gRPC client load balancing configuration:
1. application.yml: round-robin load balancing across 3 pod addresses
2. @GrpcClient with health check
3. Simulate round-robin: TrafficDistributor tracks which pod each call hits
4. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): traffic distributed, unhealthy pod excluded
hints: round_robin policy: gRPC built-in load balancing strategy
dns:///: uses DNS resolution for Kubernetes service discovery
index.getAndIncrement() % healthy.size(): modulo for circular round-robin

exercises.title: Complete M28 T2 Spring Boot gRPC synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build complete M28 T2 synthesis:
1. @GrpcService with Spring DI
2. processPayment with INVALID_ARGUMENT validation
3. getPayment with NOT_FOUND handling
4. Logging interceptor
5. Client with DEADLINE_EXCEEDED retry
6. @SpringBootTest (4 tests): service, not-found, invalid, client-retry
exercises.starter_code: // Complete M28 T2 Spring Boot gRPC synthesis
hints: TestObserver: reuse the same helper class from earlier exercises
FAILED_PRECONDITION vs INVALID_ARGUMENT: precondition = state issue; invalid_arg = request issue
retry on DEADLINE_EXCEEDED only: UNAVAILABLE don't retry
